summary: German Renaissance painter and court painter to the Electors of Saxony. Close friend of Martin Luther and prolific portraitist of the reformer. Signed works with a winged serpent emblem. Key works: Portraits of Martin Luther, The Stag Hunt of Elector Frederick the Wise, Judith with the Head of Holofernes, Wittenberg Altarpiece, The Judgment of Paris, Venus and Cupid with Honeycomb.

works.name: Portraits of Martin Luther
works.description: Many portraits of Luther in his characteristic floppy black hat. Also painted Luther's wife Katharina von Bora and his parents Hans and Margarethe. Cranach shared his printing presses with Luther and made 22 woodcuts for Luther's German Bible translation.

clues.clue: Many portraits of Luther in floppy black hat; also painted wife Katharina von Bora, parents Hans and Margarethe
examples: most famous for creating multiple portraits of his friend Martin Luther
A black hat and a coat are often worn by Katharina von Bora's husband in many of his portraits
This man appears in a black robe with a floppy hat in many portraits by his friend Lucas Cranach the Elder

clues.clue: Shared printing presses with Martin Luther; made 22 woodcuts for Luther's German Bible translation
examples: An artist represented by a winged serpent shared his printing presses with this man
Cranach also made 22 woodcuts for the Bible translation by what German reformer?

works.name: Judith with the Head of Holofernes
works.description: Several versions depicting Judith 'dressed to kill' in contemporary garb with jeweled necklaces and ornate hats. She wears a large red hat with feathers (also described as a big red feathered hat), white gloves with gold rings, and has a smug, sly smile. Another version shows steel gloves and court clothes. Part of the 'Power of Women' motif (along with Phyllis Riding Aristotle and Virgil in his basket). Art historians have connected Cranach's Judith paintings to Luther's ideological struggle against Charles V.

clues.clue: Judith 'dressed to kill' with jeweled necklaces; wears big red hat with large feathers; white gloves with gold rings; contemporary garb with smug, sly smile
examples: several paintings of Judith with the head of Holofernes, in which Judith wears a big red hat with feathers
This figure was depicted in contemporary garb with a smug, sly smile and a red hat with large feather by Lucas Cranach the Elder

Cranach the Elder depicted this woman holding up a sword and 'dressed to kill' wearing jeweled necklaces and ornate hats

clues.clue: Judith wears steel gloves and court clothes in another version

clues.clue: Part of the 'Power of Women' motif (also includes Phyllis Riding Aristotle and Virgil in his basket)
examples: Judith with the head of Holofernes; 'Power of Women' motif

Depictions of Phyllis riding Aristotle were examples of this topos popular in Northern Renaissance art. This motif is also exemplified by paintings of Virgil in his basket and Judith with the head of Holofernes

works.name: The Stag Hunt of Elector Frederick the Wise
works.description: Archers lie in wait behind bushes while deer are chased across a river. A large party waits in a boat. The artist's winged snake emblem appears on a tree in the foreground. Set near Hartenfels Castle. A woman probably identified as Sibylle, wife of John Frederick the Magnanimous, steadies a crossbow to shoot at one of the deer.

clues.clue: Archers in bushes, deer chased across river, large party in boat; winged snake emblem on a tree in the foreground

clues.clue: Set near Hartenfels Castle; woman probably Sibylle (wife of John Frederick the Magnanimous) steadies a crossbow

works.name: Wittenberg Altarpiece
works.description: Central Last Supper panel shows Jesus dining with Germans; Cranach inserted a self-portrait as a waiter and a portrait of a priest as one of the apostles. Bottom panel depicts the crucifixion between a preacher and his audience. Joseph Koerner's book The Reformation of the Image analyzes it, arguing it 'negates sacred geography, revealing church as activity, not place.' Companion to the Weimar Altarpiece (completed by his son Lucas Cranach the Younger).

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Court painter to Electors of Saxony (Frederick the Wise, etc.). Signed with bat-winged snake/serpent carrying a ruby ring (coat of arms from Frederick III; later changed to single crow's wing after son Hans's death). Close friend of Luther. Sometimes Mayor of Wittenberg. Shares feast day with Durer (April 6) in Lutheran/Episcopal churches. Pupil of his: Albrecht Altdorfer. Also painted Cardinal Albert von Brandenburg as Saint Jerome (despite Lutheran allegiance; composition derived from Durer's woodcut). Painted Phyllis Riding Aristotle and many other 'Power of Women' scenes. Saint Eustace: kneeling before stag with cross on antlers. Other works: The Judgment of Paris, Venus and Cupid with Honeycomb, Law and Gospel, The Fountain of Youth, Nymph of the Spring, Melancholia, Weimar Altarpiece, Adam and Eve against black background, Crucifixion (1503).

clues.clue: Signed works with bat-winged snake/serpent carrying ruby ring in its mouth (coat of arms from Frederick the Wise/III); later changed to single crow's wing after son Hans's death
examples: He signed his paintings with a winged snake emblem bestowed on him by Frederick III

comprehensive_summary:
Lucas Cranach the Elder (1472–1553) was a German Renaissance painter and court painter to the Electors of Saxony, most commonly identified by his many portraits of Martin Luther and his distinctive bat-winged serpent signature emblem.

He signed his works with a bat-winged snake carrying a ruby ring — a coat of arms bestowed by Frederick the Wise (Frederick III). After his son Hans died, the emblem was changed to a single crow's wing. He was attached to Frederick the Wise's court from 1504, was sometimes Mayor of Wittenberg, and was a close personal friend of Luther. He painted Luther many times in a characteristic floppy black hat and black robe, also portraying Luther's wife Katharina von Bora and his parents. Cranach shared his printing presses with Luther and provided 22 woodcuts for Luther's German Bible translation. This figure appears with a swan in many depictions (due to a prophecy at the Council of Constance). Despite his Lutheran allegiance, Cranach accepted a commission to paint Cardinal Albert von Brandenburg as Saint Jerome, deriving his composition from Durer's woodcut of the saint. He also painted Durer's rival.

His Judith with the Head of Holofernes series depicts Judith 'dressed to kill' in contemporary dress with jeweled necklaces, ornate hats, a smug sly smile — most recognizably wearing a large red hat with feathers and white gloves with gold rings. Another version shows steel gloves and court clothes. These are part of his 'Power of Women' (Weibermacht) motif, which also includes Phyllis Riding Aristotle and Virgil in his basket. Art historians have connected Cranach's Judith interest to Luther's ideological struggle against Charles V.

The Stag Hunt of Elector Frederick the Wise shows archers in bushes, deer chased across a river, a large party in a boat, the artist's winged snake emblem visible on a tree in the foreground, set near Hartenfels Castle; a woman probably identified as Sibylle (wife of John Frederick the Magnanimous) steadies a crossbow.

The Wittenberg Altarpiece's central Last Supper shows Jesus dining with Germans, includes Cranach's self-portrait as a waiter and a portrait of a priest as one of the apostles; its bottom panel depicts the Crucifixion between a preacher (Luther) and his audience. Joseph Koerner's The Reformation of the Image analyzes it, arguing it 'negates sacred geography, revealing church as activity, not place.'

Other major works: The Judgment of Paris (Mercury holds a glass sphere, Paris in red cape and armor); Venus and Cupid with Honeycomb (Cupid holds honeycomb while attacked by bees, 'complaining' to Venus; a sign on a tree moralizes on pleasure and pain; Cranach's nude Venuses wear nothing but jeweled chokers and large elaborate hats with pompoms; his Eves are indistinguishable from his Venuses); Law and Gospel (made in consultation with Philip Melanchthon; Master IW's rendition of the brazen serpent borrowed the tent-filled background from Cranach's version); The Fountain of Youth (carts of old women to a central rectangular pool); Melancholia (woman in red dress whittles a stick while 15 babies/nude infants dance beside her); Weimar Altarpiece (blood of Christ lands on the artist's own head; completed by his son Lucas Cranach the Younger); Nymph of the Spring (Latin: 'I am a nymph of the sacred fountain, do not disturb my sleep'); Adam and Eve against a black background; and the Crucifixion (1503) with a turbulent landscape, Jesus in profile off to the right (focus on Mary looking up), a knight in a feathered hat, and a soldier in pink full body armor — now in the Alte Pinakothek, Munich. Albrecht Altdorfer was a pupil of Cranach. He shares a feast day with Durer on April 6 in Lutheran and Episcopal churches. Ernst Kirchner was inspired by Cranach's Venus with transparent shawl.
